summary: A brief overview of Andrew Carnegie's 'Gospel of Wealth' philosophy, emphasizing his belief that surplus wealth carries a responsibility to serve the community. It highlights his philanthropic focus on providing opportunities through education and institutions rather than direct handouts.

principles.statement: Surplus wealth comes with a responsibility to help others.
principles.rationale: Wealth must serve the community, which is why Carnegie pledged to give his fortune away.

principles.statement: Offer a hand upward instead of giving handouts.
principles.rationale: Investing in education and institutions provides the source of opportunity that allows people to rise.

principles.statement: Have a noble motive in life by trying to make the world better than you found it.
principles.rationale: It provides a noble motive in life and serves the community.
principles.source_quote: to try and make the world in some way better than you found it.

mental_models.name: Hand Upward vs. Handout
mental_models.description: A philanthropic lens that prioritizes building infrastructure for self-improvement over direct charity.
mental_models.example: Investing in free public libraries, universities, scientific research, and institutions for peace.

signature_quotes.text: The man who dies great dies.
signature_quotes.context: From his gospel of wealth (transcribed exactly as written in the source text).

anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It does not provide the structural opportunity (like education) that allows people to rise on their own.
